Name: pac_bayes_spectral_stability
Title: PAC-Bayes Frameworks for Spectral Stability Bounds in Low-Latency Machine Learning Inference
Short Hypothesis: PAC-Bayes frameworks can be utilized to derive spectral stability bounds for low-latency machine learning inference, enabling the development of rigorous, bounded-overhead protocols for latency-critical applications.
Related Work: Recent works such as SNIP, AMP-ViT, and EMQ have explored adaptive mixed-precision training and quantization techniques for efficient model deployment. However, these approaches often rely on heuristic-based methods or require significant computational overhead. In contrast, our proposal leverages PAC-Bayes frameworks to provide a theoretically grounded approach to spectral stability bounds, ensuring rigorous and reproducible results.
Abstract: This proposal introduces a novel approach to low-latency machine learning inference by leveraging PAC-Bayes frameworks to derive spectral stability bounds. By combining these bounds with explicit complementary pathways, we can develop rigorous, bounded-overhead protocols for latency-critical applications. Our approach prioritizes empirical validation, mechanistic transparency, and reproducible, training-minimized designs, ensuring that our results are both theoretically sound and practically relevant. We will conduct experiments to validate our proposal, using metrics such as latency, accuracy, and computational overhead to evaluate the effectiveness of our approach.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Derivation of spectral stability bounds using PAC-Bayes frameworks
Experiment 2: Evaluation of bounded-overhead protocols for latency-critical applications
Experiment 3: Comparison with state-of-the-art approaches (e.g., SNIP, AMP-ViT, EMQ) in terms of latency, accuracy, and computational overhead
Risk Factors and Limitations: Theoretical complexity of PAC-Bayes frameworks may limit their applicability to certain domains
Computational overhead of deriving spectral stability bounds may be significant
Potential for overfitting or underfitting in the development of bounded-overhead protocols
